sentences.text: Well, like I told you, I met my father in Auschwitz.
When they brought us and they shaved us, and everything they did, they did with the men the same thing.
We put on those clothes, the striped clothes and everything, and uh, they were like in the other side.
We were on this side, they were on the other side.
But my mother and my sister, [INAUDIBLE] they weren't there already.
They weren't there.
But my brother, Yittel, and my father were together.
And he told me that I should watch over my sister because I'm the older one, and also say that, you know you- you gonna work anything.
Whatever, you know-- I should always say that I know how to work, I know how to do things, whatever they ask me [INAUDIBLE], and keep on and watch over of my sister and myself.
That's in Auschwitz.
That's exactly when we came.
They shaved us, so we-- before, we took us to the- to the barracks.
What do you call it?
Uh, it took 10 minutes to speak to him.
They took him away already.
No, face to face.
My father looked horrible.
He had a little beard, like he said.
It was shaved up.
Everything was shaved.
He had this round hat-- what they were wearing with the stripes, you know.
That's what we had.
Uh, just a long dress, like a- a long, horrible [NON-ENGLISH], what you call it.
I don't know.
Black, and we had like a- a German thing on it.
You know, like the German thing.
That's what we had on it, on the front.
It was horrible.
No, there was no undergarments.
Nobody had undergarments.
What people did a lot, they took a piece from the dress and they put it on their head.
They made a little-- a little kerchief from it.
In the blocks, and I was in A place.
[INAUDIBLE] but Europe we call it 'ah' and E and A.
I was in A, that's called 'ah'-- A place.
I was very close to the chimneys, to the all of those gas, to all those things.
I saw everything going on.
Nobody had to tell me anything.
I knew what's happening.
I didn't ask nobody where my mother-- my mother is, or my sisters are, or whatever.
I knew that they are not alive.
That screaming was unbearable, and the smell was unbearable.
And, uh, well, we were outside, and especially it was warm.
Especially when it's-- people had, um, um, boils.
You know, they were longer there, a little bit, than us, too.
And the flies used to go on it, and that's what they liked.
Perfect, they loved it.
He says, you have to go sit there where the sun is.
And they were full of boils with flies.
With, um-- and every day, there were dead people.
And when the people came in, like from-- like [INAUDIBLE], they had music.
They had beautiful music playing.
It's a band.
A band, they were playing all of it.
They were playing music.
When they put people in the gas, they play music.
They had their people from before.
Some people, you know, they knew-- they knew how to play the instrument, and they were, before, already in Auschwitz.
So, they were playing, our people.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] But, you had to.
That's how you save your life.
No, it was drums and, um, and-- uh, marching, a marching music.
And meanwhile, they burned the people there, and they gassed them.
It was-- that screaming, the things-- I can't describe it.
And if I wouldn't be there, maybe I wouldn't believe it that it could be like that, something.
Of course, people died from typhus.
You couldn't go in the bathroom when you wanted.
When they wanted, they take you.
It was also like a latrine, you know.
And the water, you can't drink there.
It was full of poison from those-- they brought in the gas, and you know, the people that they burned all the time-- that water, you couldn't drink.
And I drank a little bit when I went in, when they took me there.
How could you be without water?
Absolutely, except coffee, black coffee.
And that was in the morning, and also, you have to bring it yourself.
Everybody morning, they started to hit with a stick, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And they were screaming, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and they were yelling and telling you left to right.
Every place, it was at the [INAUDIBLE], somebody else had to go bring the coffee.
It was that big, and heavy, you know, but we want coffee.
And then we were standing in the row, and there was a red plate, and that plate was coffee.
Also, they took put a medication-- a lot of people don't know that medication.
These people don't know of it, didn't know, it's called Brom.
The name was Brom.
They put it in, every morning, in the coffee.
[SIGHS] I don't know, people didn't have their period.
I don't know if this made it, or we didn't have food enough.
We weren't nourishment- nourished enough.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we, uh-- so everybody had a sip.
It went back and forth.
We were five in a row.
Everybody had a sip.
It went back, forth until it was empty, and nobody was allowed to drink a sip, two sips, [INAUDIBLE] because there wasn't enough.
And then, after that coffee, they stand us in the row, nude.
Mengele came to check if we were r-- we're ready to go to work.
But that was going on about six weeks time.
I know his face.
He was the doctor who said-- who said who's going left and who's going right there.
There were some people took out right there, and they knew they're never going to come back.
And they didn't.
And that-- in the morning, when you-- first of all, you stand nude there.
And it was cold already also, it wasn't that warm already.
Um, you were so afraid.
Who knows what he's going to tell you?
If you were selected for work, or you selected somewhere else to the left side.
You know, it was a horrible thing.
This was a terrible experience.
And also, the bonfires in Auschwitz was-- I think it was the most terrible, terrible experience in my life, I guess.
He didn't touch us.
He just looked top to bottom, and that was it.
It didn't take long.
He went through us, that's it.
Well, um, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] there was a bunk-- it was bunks, you know-- and that bunk was about four people.
It was not so big.
I mean, you-- that was our place, there, to sleep, but there was a lot of bunks there.
And sometimes at night we had nightmares.
There was a woman walking around.
She took a piece from her-- she had a child, they took away her child.
They took away-- She took a piece from her dress, she made a doll and was going around middle of the night.
She used to scream horrible things, nightmares.

And the SS hear that there is a big screaming, and one of them came and he says, like that, if you aren't going to tell us who was screaming, who made that big noise, all of you gonna go to the gas chamber.
We have to find out.
You have to tell us who was that woman.
And who wants to tell?
But we had to.
I mean, I didn't.
I would never.
And they took her out, and they gassed her also.
She went with her drug.
It was awful.
It was awful.
She wasn't the only one.
There was a lot of them like that.
And also, they give you a piece of bread, let's say, at night.
I mean, I didn't talk yet for lunch, but we had-- there was coffee in the morning.
But lunchtime, they used to give us soup, one big spoon of soup.
You know, with special use for soup.
And that was cabbage.
Sometimes you could see little things are crawling there around.
At the same time, they used to come lunchtime and take a little bit of blood from the people.
Two doctors, three doctors came, and they take blood.
They made experiment, I guess.
There was one girl-- she was a very strong, husky girl-- and she was not bad.
She hold herself not bad, because she was fat before.
We were skinnier already.
And she had-- I guess that she had a rare blood that they needed or something, and they took from her the blood until she was dead.
That's it.
And there was horrible things.
We were afraid that if we go, that we're going to get that little bit soup, they're going to take blood.
They give you-- give you another piece of bread because they took blood, and they give you a piece of salami.
It was horse-- horse meat.
And that was-- they told us, that's going to bring you back your blood that we took from you.
And I was going on for two weeks like that.
I  was sometimes hiding.
Sometimes I didn't go for that-- for that, uh, soup, because I was always afraid how much blood they're going to take out from me.
They used to take tubes, sometimes six, seven at one time.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And, um, that was going on still, we had to stand for Mengele until they send us away for work.
No, I got the number when I went to work.
Three million, three thousand, three hundred, thirty three-- I remember that. 3, 3.
When I went to Breslau, before I went to work, that was given for us.
And they took us to work, they took us to Breslau.
That was Auschwitz from Breslau, was-- it was far.
They took us by the wagons, the cattle.
[SIGH] Birkenau was a-- there was no working there.
That was a death camp.
And what you did was sitting around and waiting for your death, and that's it.
There was-- there was screaming, there  was crying, there was all kind of things going on, terrible things.
Every day you see people, they were dead, and, uh-- and you see they took them away.
They took them to, uh-- to the crematorium.
Um, um, it was-- every single day we saw the people are coming, and they tortured you.
They tortured you, that's it.
If somebody was sick, he didn't say that he's sick.
He's afraid, but then he died anyway.
And a lot of time, we were standing for Mengele.
And a lot of girls, they couldn't stand even.
We just hold them, you know, while Mengele went by.
We were afraid that they're finished, because if you couldn't stand, that's it.
Finished.
There was also what happened to me once.
I don't know if I should say it.
It's a horrible thing.
I needed to go.
Here they take you, I don't know, in two hours, or three hours, or whatever they want.
And a before, I was standing for Mengele, and I needed to urinate.
And I did.
I didn't have panties, I didn't have nothing.
Okay.
And what do you think what they did?
They brought a little, uh, a spoon from somewhere, and I had to drink it.
And they hit me, also.
Not Mengele, the other ones there.
Some of them were very rude.
There were Polish people there, also, and they were rude.
They used to hit us, also, went to the [INAUDIBLE]  latrine.
They used to hit us, also, there.
Sometime you have to go, and you run there.
It was far enough to go there, and if they saw you come alone they didn't bring you.
They didn't let you go in.
They hit you, and you had to go back.
[PAUSES] I can't describe that, this is-- this was, like you say, it was a Gehenna.
It was.
It was, uh-- when we took us to work we were happy, already.
We thought we were liberated.
Everybody had a plate.
Well, this was in Poland and this was in Germany.
And I really don't know.
I can't-- I can't tell you.
But we were on this thing, I think we were going, um, two days on a train.
Not a train, but a cattle train.
My sister was with me.
She was lucky that one time he said, ah, she's young.
She can't work.
And he put her on this side.
I-- I pushed her-- I schlepped her back.
I said, and she's staying with me.
He said she looks too young for him, that she won't-- she can't work.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I pulled her back.
He didn't see.
On the side, I pulled her back.
She was standing the side from me.
And, ah-- the work wasn't easy.
We had a little bit different food there.
Well, um, not too much.
There was also a little piece of bread and a coffee.
That was morning.
Actually, we had to-- we had to march three kilometers to our work where that factory was.
We had to march there, back and forth.
We had come for lunch back, and then go back again.
There was also night shift and Tag shift.
That's called night you work, and you work at daytime.
So, in daytime, they let you sleep a little bit.
You know, not too much, but two o'clock.
I mean, you got your lunch, two o'clock you have to be ready-- ready to march to work.
We made, musi- um, Munition.
What was your specific job?
My specific job was, um, I had a machine.
There is some kind of a paper what was rolling in that machine.
That was also like for shooting.
Uh, it wasn't a plain paper, something.
And from that, you have to work out I don't know how many things, you know, how many pieces.
It was like rolling up in that machine.
It was like, uh, yellowish, it was a hard thing.
And that machine rolled it up, and we had to put it in a side and then bring it in a place where they put it together.
And my sister did the same thing with a big machine.
It was hard work.
And one night, I don't know, I was tired, and there was German girls watching over us.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They were watching us, how we're working.
If we're working fast enough.
I fell asleep for one second, and they took me out from there and I had to dig for four weeks.
And they didn't-- they didn't take me to work, but I was working outside.
I had to dig graves for the people who were dying for four weeks.
That was, um-- I don't know, September, end of September, something.
October?
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] October, it must have been October.
I remember it was cold, not very warm.
Um, they gave us very little to eat.
I mean, it was different than in Auschwitz, but the food was still very little.
We got that piece of bread, but it was like a piece of-- like a rock.
And you got that coffee, and that was it.
You had to work for that.
At lunchtime, they gave you-- they gave us a little bit more.
Like a soup, and there was potatoes already, and sometimes a little horse meat in it.
[INAUDIBLE] That was it.
And suppertime is, again, a piece of bread, coffee, black coffee, that's it.
Once, the, uh-- the foreman couldn't understand what's happening to such young girls that they're dying.
So, uh, one of the SS-- because the foreman told him that those people can't work, they, uh-- they haven't got enough food.
He saw that we are-- we can't go on.
So one SS came and he checked out, and we got a little bit more food with that.
And, uh, we were working.
And a lot of people died.
At the end of the war, there was 12 people left from my 2,000 people who were together, working.
And that was still, you know, marching, marching, a lot of marching.
Uh, they weren't-- when we left, before we left from Breslau, we were working.
They never took us Sunday to work.
We weren't working Sunday, because it was closed.
But this time, they took us to work Sunday morning, and at-- and they brought us back, and they were crazy.
They were shooting left to right.
They didn't know what to do with themselves, because they knew that it's over.
You know, because in Breslau, when we left in the morning at one o'clock, the Russians were there.
That happened-- that must happened, um, in the end of November, sometime then.
No, that was later, I think.
I remember we were marching.
It was very, very cold, and lots of snow.
That must have been in December.
Pardon me?
Still in 1944.
